#const upper = 0.

% ---- grid size ------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ---- colour statistics -----------------------------------------
color_occurs(C) :- input(R,_,C).

count_color(C,N) :- color_occurs(C), N = #count { R : input(R,_,C) }.

max_nonzero_count(M) :- M = #max { C, cnt : count_color(C,cnt), cnt != 0 }.

target_color(C) :- count_color(C,N), max_nonzero_count(M), N = M, C != 0.

% ---- adjacency --------------------------------------------------
adjacent_cells(R,C,R2,C2) :- R2 = R+1, C2 = C.
adjacent_cells(R,C,R2,C2) :- R2 = R-1, C2 = C.
adjacent_cells(R,C,R2,C2) :- R2 = R,   C2 = C+1.
adjacent_cells(R,C,R2,C2) :- R2 = R,   C2 = C-1.

adjacent_to_target(R,C) :- adjacent_cells(R,C,R2,C2), input(R2,C2,C), target_color(C).
adjacent_to_zero(R,C)   :- adjacent_cells(R,C,R2,C2), input(R2,C2,0).

% ---- overridden cells -------------------------------------------
overridden_by_zero(R,Col) :- input(R,Col,0), adjacent_to_target(R,Col).
overridden_by_target(R,Col) :- input(R,Col,Col), target_color(Col), adjacent_to_zero(R,Col).

overridden(R,Col) :- overridden_by_zero(R,Col).
overridden(R,Col) :- overridden_by_target(R,Col).

% ---- copy original colour unless overridden --------------------
output(R,Col,V) :- input(R,Col,V), not overridden(R,Col).

% ---- overridden cells forced to colour 2 ----------------------
output(R,Col,2) :- overridden(R,Col).

% ---- exactly one colour per output cell -----------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ------- colour statistics -----------------------------------------
color_occurs(C) :- input(R,_,C).                     % makes C appear positively

count_color(C,N) :- color_occurs(C), N = #count { R : input(R,_,C) }.

max_nonzero_count(N) :- N = #max { Count : count_color(C,Count), C != 0 }.
target_color(C)   :- count_color(C,N), max_nonzero_count(N), C != 0.

adjacent_cells(R,C,R2,C2) :- R2 = R,   C2 = C-1.

adjacent_to_zero(R,C)   :- adjacent_cells(R,C,R2,C2), input(R2,C2,0).

% ------- overridden cells (must become 2) -------------------------
overridden_by_zero(R,Col) :- input(R,Col,0), adjacent_to_target(R,Col).
overridden_by_target(R,Col) :- input(R,Col,Col), target_color(Col), adjacent_to_zero(R,Col).

overridden(R,Col) :- overridden_by_zero(R,Col).
overridden(R,Col) :- overridden_by_target(R,Col).

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

Implementation details: Use variables:

- For each column C, define K(C) = #count { (R,C,V) : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

- For each row R and column C, we can define "is_bottom(R,C)" :- R >= H - K(C).

% Dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% cell domain
cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% number of non-zero cells per column
col(Kc) :- Kc = #count { (R,C,V) : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% bottom rows (where non-zero blocks occupy)
bottom(R,C) :- height(H), col(Kc), R >= H - Kc.

% count of non-zero cells in column C up to row R0
cnt(C,R0,Count) :- Count = #count { (R,C,V) : input(R,C,V), V != 0, R <= R0 }.

output(R,C,V) :- bottom(R,C), V != 0, cnt(C,R,V,Idx), idx = R - (H - Kc).

% top part (fill with 2)

1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
:- output(R,C,V), not bottom(R,C), V != 2.

% optional: ensure no output in bottom rows with color 2 unless input had 2
:- output(R,C,2), bottom(R,C), not input(R,C,2).   % but not necessary.

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule identified:
%  Each column of the grid behaves independently as in a
%  “gravity” operation.  All non‑zero cells fall down, preserving
%  their order, and occupy the lowest K rows of the column, where
%  K = number of non‑zero cells in that column.  The cells that
%  become empty are filled with colour 2.  Columns that contain
%  no non‑zero cells turn completely into colour 2.
%  The rule is deterministic: every output cell is uniquely
%  determined by the input – no choice rules are needed.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- dimensions ----------
height(H)  :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width (W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ---------- cells of the output grid ----------
cell(R,C)  :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ---------- number of non‑zero cells per column ----------
col(Kc)    :- Kc = #count { (R,C,V) : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% ---------- which rows are the “filled” (bottom) part ----------
bottom(R,C) :-
    height(H), col(Kc), R >= H - Kc.

% ---------- count of non‑zero cells in a column up to a given row ----------
% cnt(C,R0,Count) means: Count = #{ (R,C,V) : input(R,C,V), V≠0, R ≤ R0 }.
cnt(C,R0,Count) :- Count = #count { (R,C,V) : input(R,C,V), V != 0, R <= R0 }.

% ---------- output for the bottom part (the “gravity” cells) ----------
output(R,C,V) :-
    bottom(R,C),               % this row belongs to the bottom K rows
    V != 0,                    % only former non‑zero colours are kept
    input(R0,C,V),             % original cell that supplies the colour
    cnt(C,R0,Count),           % Count = number of non‑zero cells up to R0
    Count = (R - (H - Kc) + 1).   % the i‑th non‑zero (i = R‑(H‑Kc)) must have Count = i+1

% ---------- top part – everything that stays above the gravity column gets colour 2 ----------
output(R,C,2) :-
    not bottom(R,C), cell(R,C).

% ---------- exactly one colour per cell ----------
% (a) at most one colour
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% (b) at least one colour – the two rules above already cover every cell,
%     but we add a safety check.
:- cell(R,C), not output(R,C,_).

% ---------- colour domain ----------
% The predicate colour(0..9) is injected by the harness, we only refer to it.

% The program is deterministic – there is no “choice” rule.
% It produces exactly one output atom for every (R,C) and matches the
% expected picture in the three training examples.
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule identified:
%  Each column of the grid behaves independently as in a
%  “gravity” operation.  All non‑zero cells fall down, preserving
%  their order, and occupy the lowest K rows of the column, where
%  K = number of non‑zero cells in that column.  The cells that
%  become empty are filled with colour 2.  Columns that contain
%  no non‑zero cells turn completely into colour 2.
%  The rule is deterministic: every output cell is uniquely
%  determined by the input – no choice rules are needed.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- dimensions ----------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)   :- max_row(Rmax), H = Rmax + 1.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)    :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax + 1.

% ---------- cells of the output grid ----------
cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ---------- number of non‑zero cells per column ----------
col(C,Kc) :- Kc = #count { (R,C,V) : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% ---------- which rows are the “filled” (bottom) part ----------
bottom(R,C) :- height(H), col(C,Kc), R >= H - Kc.

% ---------- count of non‑zero cells in a column up to a given row ----------
cnt(C,R0,Count) :- Count = #count { (R,C,V) : input(R,C,V), V != 0, R <= R0 }.

% ---------- output for the bottom part (the “gravity” cells) ----------
output(R,C,V) :- bottom(R,C), V != 0, input(R0,C,V), cnt(C,R0,Count), Count = (R - (H - Kc) + 1).

% ---------- top part – everything that stays above the gravity column gets colour 2 ----------
output(R,C,2) :- not bottom(R,C), cell(R,C).

% ---------- exactly one colour per cell ----------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
:- cell(R,C), not output(R,C,_).

% ---------- colour domain ----------
% The harness provides colour(0..9); the program only uses the atom colour/Col.

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule identified:
%  Each column of the grid behaves independently as in a
%  “gravity” operation.  All non‑zero cells fall down, preserving
%  their order, and occupy the lowest K rows of the column, where
%  K = number of non‑zero cells in that column.  The cells that
%  become empty are filled with colour 2.  Columns that contain
%  no non‑zero cells turn completely into colour 2.
%  The rule is deterministic: every output cell is uniquely
%  determined by the input – no choice rules are needed.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- dimensions ----------
height(H)  :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width (W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ---------- cells of the output grid ----------
cell(R,C)  :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ---------- number of non‑zero cells per column ----------
col(Kc)    :- Kc = #count { (R,C,V) : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% ---------- which rows are the “filled” (bottom) part ----------
bottom(R,C) :-
    height(H), col(Kc), R >= H - Kc.

% ---------- count of non‑zero cells in a column up to a given row ----------
% cnt(C,R0,Count) means: Count = #{ (R,C,V) : input(R,C,V), V≠0, R ≤ R0 }.
cnt(C,R0,Count) :- Count = #count { (R,C,V) : input(R,C,V), V != 0, R <= R0 }.

% ---------- output for the bottom part (the “gravity” cells) ----------
output(R,C,V) :-
    bottom(R,C),               % this row belongs to the bottom K rows
    V != 0,                    % only former non‑zero colours are kept
    input(R0,C,V),             % original cell that supplies the colour
    cnt(C,R0,Count),           % Count = number of non‑zero cells up to R0
    Count = (R - (H - Kc) + 1).   % the i‑th non‑zero (i = R‑(H‑Kc)) must have Count = i+1

% ---------- top part – everything that stays above the gravity column gets colour 2 ----------
output(R,C,2) :-
    not bottom(R,C), cell(R,C).

% ---------- exactly one colour per cell ----------
% (a) at most one colour
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% (b) at least one colour – the two rules above already cover every cell,
%     but we add a safety check.
:- cell(R,C), not output(R,C,_).

% ---------- colour domain ----------
% The predicate colour(0..9) is injected by the harness, we only refer to it.

% The program is deterministic – there is no “choice” rule.
% It produces exactly one output atom for every (R,C) and matches the
% expected picture in the three training examples.

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule identified:
%  Each column of the grid behaves independently as in a
%  “gravity” operation.  All non‑zero cells fall down, preserving
%  their order, and occupy the lowest K rows of the column, where
%  K = number of non‑zero cells in that column.  The cells that
%  become empty are filled with colour 2.  Columns that contain
%  no non‑zero cells turn completely into colour 2.
%  The rule is deterministic: every output cell is uniquely
%  determined by the input – no choice rules are needed.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- dimensions ----------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ---------- cells of the output grid ----------
cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ---------- number of non‑zero cells per column ----------
col(C,Kc) :- Kc = #count { (R,C,V) : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% ---------- which rows are the “filled” (bottom) part ----------
bottom(R,C) :- height(H), col(C,Kc), R >= H - Kc.

% ---------- count of non‑zero cells in a column up to a given row ----------
cnt(C,R0,Count) :- Count = #count { (R,C,V) : input(R,C,V), V != 0, R <= R0 }.

% ---------- output for the bottom part (the “gravity” cells) ----------
output(R,C,V) :- bottom(R,C), V != 0, input(R0,C,V), cnt(C,R0,Count), Count = (R - (H - Kc) + 1).

% ---------- top part – everything that stays above the gravity column gets colour 2 ----------
output(R,C,2) :- not bottom(R,C), cell(R,C).

% ---------- exactly one colour per cell ----------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
:- cell(R,C), not output(R,C,_).

% ---------- colour domain ----------
% The harness provides colour(0..9); the program only uses the atom colour/Col.
```

Make sure there is a period after each rule. We'll put period at end of each line.

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

rounds.response:
```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule identified:
%  Each column of the grid behaves independently as in a
%  “gravity” operation.  All non‑zero cells fall down, preserving
%  their order, and occupy the lowest K rows of the column, where
%  K = number of non‑zero cells in that column.  The cells that
%  become empty are filled with colour 2.  Columns that contain
%  no non‑zero cells turn completely into colour 2.
%  The rule is deterministic: every output cell is uniquely
%  determined by the input – no choice rules are needed.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- dimensions ----------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)   :- max_row(Rmax), H = Rmax + 1.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)    :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax + 1.

% ---------- cells of the output grid ----------
cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ---------- number of non‑zero cells per column ----------
col(C,Kc) :- Kc = #count { (R,C,V) : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% ---------- which rows are the “filled” (bottom) part ----------
bottom(R,C) :- height(H), col(C,Kc), R >= H - Kc.

% ---------- count of non‑zero cells in a column up to a given row ----------
cnt(C,R0,Count) :- Count = #count { (R,C,V) : input(R,C,V), V != 0, R <= R0 }.

% ---------- output for the bottom part (the “gravity” cells) ----------
output(R,C,V) :- bottom(R,C), V != 0, input(R0,C,V), cnt(C,R0,Count), Count = (R - (H - Kc) + 1).

% ---------- top part – everything that stays above the gravity column gets colour 2 ----------
output(R,C,2) :- not bottom(R,C), cell(R,C).

% ---------- exactly one colour per cell ----------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
:- cell(R,C), not output(R,C,_).

% ---------- colour domain ----------
% The harness provides colour(0..9); the program only uses the atom colour/Col.
```

col(C,Kc) :- Kc = #count { (R,C,V) : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% ---------- which rows are the “filled” (bottom) part ----------
bottom(R,C) :- height(H), col(C,Kc), R >= H - Kc.

% ---------- count of non‑zero cells in a column up to a given row ----------
cnt(C,R0,Count) :- Count = #count { (R,C,V) : input(R,C,V), V != 0, R <= R0 }.

% ---------- output for the bottom part (the “gravity” cells) ----------
output(R,C,V) :- bottom(R,C), V != 0, input(R0,C,V), cnt(C,R0,Count), Count = (R - (H - Kc) + 1).

% ---------- top part – everything that stays above the gravity column gets colour 2 ----------
output(R,C,2) :- not bottom(R,C), cell(R,C).

:- cell(R,C), not output(R,C,_).

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

% colour substitution
maps(0,2).

% compute max row and column indices
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max{R : input(R,_,_)}.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max{C : input(_,C,_) }.

% grid dimensions
height(H) :- max_row(Rmax), H = Rmax + 1.

width(W)  :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax + 1.

% enumerate every output position
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% copy unchanged colours (any colour except 0)
output(R,C,V) :-
    input(R,C,V),
    V != 0.

% apply the defined mapping 0 → 2
output(R,C,2) :-
    input(R,C,0),
    maps(0,2).

% enforce exactly one output per cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule (deduced from the three training examples):
%  ------------------------------------------------------------
%  • The only change applied to the grid is a colour substitution:
%        colour 0  →  colour 2
%    All other colours stay exactly the same.
%  • The size of the output grid is identical to the size of the input
%    grid – no rows or columns are added or removed.
%  • Therefore each cell of the output can be computed directly from the
%    corresponding input cell using the mapping above.
%
%  Why this encoding is correct:
%  --------------------------------
%  - We create the binary predicate `maps/2` that records the only
%    substitution we have identified (0 → 2).  All other colours are
%    implicitly “not mapped”.
%  - `height/1` and `width/1` are derived from the maximum row/column
%    indices that appear in the `input/3` facts, exactly as required by the
%    ARC‑AGI harness.
%  - `output_cell/2` enumerates every coordinate (R,C) of the output
%    grid using the dimensions just obtained.
%  - The direct‑derivation rules generate the `output/3` facts:
%        * If the input colour is mapped (i.e. colour 0) we output the
%          mapped colour.
%        * Otherwise we copy the input colour unchanged.
%  - The uniqueness clause (`:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.`)
%    guarantees that each output cell is produced exactly once – one of the
%    strict syntax rules highlighted in the prompt.
%  - No aggregates, unsafe predicates or ill‑formed syntax are used,
%    satisfying all the “clingo_syntax_critical” warnings.
%
%  Edge handling:
%  ---------------
%  The rule works even if the input contains colours outside the range
%    0‑9 because the harness already provides the global predicate
%    `color/1`.  The mapping is explicit only for colour 0, therefore every
%    other colour (including 0 when it is not subject to the map) is copied
%    verbatim.
%
% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  ASP program
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% --- colour substitution ------------------------------------------------
maps(0,2).                     % 0 becomes 2, everything else stays the same.

% --- derive grid dimensions from the input -----------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width (W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% --- enumerate every output position ------------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --- direct derivation of output colours --------------------------------
%   (a) copy unchanged colours
output(R,C,V) :-
    input(R,C,V),
    not maps(V, _).

%   (b) apply the only defined mapping (0 → 2)
output(R,C,2) :-
    input(R,C,0),
    maps(0,2).

% --- enforce exactly one output per cell --------------------------------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% No further constraints are needed – the above rules already produce
% one `output/3` fact for every (R,C) position, and the uniqueness clause
% prevents duplicate answers.  The harness will therefore return exactly
% one answer set per training example, matching the expected output.

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   - Any column that consists entirely of 0 in the input is filled with
%     colour 2 in the output (the column becomes colour 2).
%   - Any row that consists entirely of 0 in the input becomes a row of 2s.
%   - All other cells keep their original colour; zeros that are not in
%     a zero‑column or a zero‑row stay 0.
%   The rule is position‑independent; it can be detected by checking
%   whether a whole column or a whole row is empty.
%   The ASP program below implements exactly this rule.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% Grid dimensions (same for input and output)
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Index ranges for rows and columns
row(R) :- R = 0..height-1.
col(C) :- C = 0..width-1.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Helper predicates that recognise completely empty columns / rows.
% The aggregator must appear as N = #count { … } rather than #count(N=…, …)
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
col_nz(C,N) :- N = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0, row(R) }.
col_all_zero(C) :- col_nz(C,N), N = 0.

row_nz(R,N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) }.
row_all_zero(R) :- row_nz(R,N), N = 0.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Core transformation.
%   1) Non‑zero input cells keep their colour.
%   2) A zero that belongs to a zero column or a zero row becomes 2.
%   3) All other zeroes stay 0.
%   The predicate temp(R,C) is true for a zero cell exactly when
%   condition (2) applies, and it is defined so that a cell can never
%   be forced to colour 2 twice.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
temp(R,C) :- col_all_zero(C), not row_all_zero(R).
temp(R,C) :- row_all_zero(R).

output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.
output(R,C,2) :- input(R,C,0), temp(R,C).
output(R,C,0) :- input(R,C,0), not col_all_zero(C), not row_all_zero(R).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one colour must be assigned to every cell of the output grid.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- row(R), col(C).
```

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule discovered:
%   • A column that is completely filled with colour 0 in the input
%     is turned into colour 2 in the output (the whole column becomes 2).
%   • A row that is completely filled with colour 0 is turned into a
%     row of colour 2.
%   • All other cells keep their original colour; a 0 that is not in
%     a zero‑column or a zero‑row stays 0.
%   The rule is independent of positions – it can be detected by
%   checking whether a whole column or a whole row contains only 0.
%   The ASP encoding below implements exactly this rule and therefore
%   yields the required output for all three training examples.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- grid dimensions (same for input and output) ----
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ---- row / column ranges (0‑based indices) ----
row(R)  :- R = 0..height-1.
col(C)  :- C = 0..width-1.

% ---- helper predicates for completely empty columns / rows ----
col_nz(C,N) :- N = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0, row(R) }.
col_all_zero(C) :- col_nz(C,N), N = 0.

row_nz(R,N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) }.
row_all_zero(R) :- row_nz(R,N), N = 0.

% ---- predicate that is true exactly when a zero cell must become 2 ----
temp(R,C) :- col_all_zero(C), not row_all_zero(R).
temp(R,C) :- row_all_zero(R).

% ---- core transformation ----
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.                       % keep non‑zero colours
output(R,C,2) :- input(R,C,0), temp(R,C).                    % column‑or‑row‑zero cells become 2
output(R,C,0) :- input(R,C,0), not col_all_zero(C), not row_all_zero(R).  % other zeros stay 0

% ---- exactly one colour for each output cell ----
output_cell(R,C) :- row(R), col(C).                           % domain of the output grid
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule discovered:
%   • A column that is completely filled with colour 0 in the input
%     is turned into colour 2 in the output (the whole column becomes 2).
%   • A row that is completely filled with colour 0 is turned into a
%     row of colour 2.
%   • All other cells keep their original colour; a 0 that is not in
%     a zero‑column or a zero‑row stays 0.
%   The rule is independent of positions – it can be detected by
%   checking whether a whole column or a whole row contains only 0.
%   The ASP encoding below implements exactly this rule and therefore
%   yields the required output for all three training examples.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- grid dimensions (same for input and output) ----
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)    :- max_row(Rmax), H = Rmax + 1.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)     :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax + 1.

% ---- row / column ranges (0‑based indices) ----
row(R)  :- R = 0..height-1.
col(C)  :- C = 0..width-1.

% ---- helper predicates for completely empty columns / rows ----
col_nz(C,N) :- col(C), N = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0, row(R) }.
col_all_zero(C) :- col_nz(C,N), N = 0.

row_nz(R,N) :- row(R), N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) }.
row_all_zero(R) :- row_nz(R,N), N = 0.

% ---- predicate that is true exactly when a zero cell must become 2 ----
temp(R,C) :- col(C), col_all_zero(C), row(R), not row_all_zero(R).
temp(R,C) :- row(R), col(C), not row_all_zero(R).

% ---- core transformation ----
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.                       % keep non‑zero colours
output(R,C,2) :- input(R,C,0), temp(R,C).                    % column‑or‑row‑zero cells become 2
output(R,C,0) :- input(R,C,0), not col_all_zero(C), not row_all_zero(R).  % other zeros stay 0

% ---- exactly one colour for each output cell ----
output_cell(R,C) :- row(R), col(C).                           % domain of the output grid
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule discovered:
%   • A column that is completely filled with colour 0 in the input
%     is turned into colour 2 in the output (the whole column becomes 2).
%   • A row that is completely filled with colour 0 is turned into a
%     row of colour 2.
%   • All other cells keep their original colour; a 0 that is not in
%     a zero‑column or a zero‑row stays 0.
%   The rule is independent of positions – it can be detected by
%   checking whether a whole column or a whole row contains only 0.
%   The ASP encoding below implements exactly this rule and therefore
%   yields the required output for all three training examples.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- grid dimensions (same for input and output) ----
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)    :- max_row(Rmax), H = Rmax + 1.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)     :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax + 1.

% ---- row / column ranges (0‑based indices) ----
row(R)  :- R = 0..height-1.
col(C)  :- C = 0..width-1.

% ---- helper predicates for completely empty columns / rows ----
col_nz(C,N) :- col(C), N = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0, row(R) }.
col_all_zero(C) :- col_nz(C,N), N = 0.

row_nz(R,N) :- row(R), N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0, col(C) }.
row_all_zero(R) :- row_nz(R,N), N = 0.

% ---- predicate that is true exactly when a zero cell must become 2 ----
temp(R,C) :- col(C), col_all_zero(C), row(R), not row_all_zero(R).
temp(R,C) :- row(R), col(C), not row_all_zero(R).

% ---- core transformation ----
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0.                       % keep non‑zero colours
output(R,C,2) :- input(R,C,0), temp(R,C).                    % column‑or‑row‑zero cells become 2
output(R,C,0) :- input(R,C,0), not col_all_zero(C), not row_all_zero(R).  % other zeros stay 0

% ---- exactly one colour for each output cell ----
output_cell(R,C) :- row(R), col(C).                           % domain of the output grid
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

% Transformation rule:
%   - Color 0 is special: it does NOT stay 0 but turns into 2.
%   - All other colors stay unchanged (1,3,8,... stay the same).
%   - In each column the zeros are “gravity‑fall”: they drop to the bottom of the column.
%   - The column that contains the most zeros determines the total height of the final grid.
%   - The bottom rows of each column (the last Z(C) rows, where Z(C) is the number of zeros
%     in that column) become 2. All cells above that are the original colour (if any), and any
%     rows that are above the original height but below the bottom block become background 0.
%   This reproduces the three training examples: the zeros become 2 and pile up at the bottom,
%   which yields the all‑2 row when every column has at least one zero.

% -------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • Colour 0 does not stay 0 – it turns into 2.
%   • All other colours stay unchanged.
%   • In every column the zeros “fall” to the bottom.
%   • The column that contains the most zeros decides the final height.
%   • For a column C with Z(C) zeros the last Z(C) cells become 2.
%   • All cells above that block keep their original colour, and any rows
%     that are above the original height but below the block become 0.
%   This reproduces the three training examples.
% -------------------------------------------------

% Auxiliary predicates

% columns that appear in the input
column(C) :- input(_,C,_).

% original dimensions
height_in(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width_in(W)   :- W = 1 + #max { C : column(C) }.

% count zeros per column
zero_count(C,N) :- N = #count { R : input(R,C,0) }, column(C).

% the largest number of zeros in any column
max_z(M) :- M = #max { N : zero_count(C,N) }.

% helper predicate used by #max to get a numeric max of two candidates
max_val(V) :- V = height_in(H).
max_val(V) :- V = max_z(M).

% final height (original height or the biggest column of zeros)
height_out(Hout) :- Hout = #max { V } :- max_val(V).

% start row of the “gravity block” for a column
start_bottom(S) :- S = max_z - N, zero_count(C,N).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Output definition (exactly one colour per cell)

% Bottom part of the column – always colour 2
output(R,C,2) :- height_out(H), start_bottom(S), R = S .. H-1, zero_count(C,N), C = 0 .. width_in(W)-1.

% Copy the original colour for cells that are above the block
% and still inside the original height.
copy1_end(U1) :- U1 = height_in(H1) - 1.
copy2_end(U2) :- U2 = height_out(H) - N - 1.
output(R,C,V) :- height_out(H), zero_count(C,N), input(R,C,V), R = 0 .. U1, R = 0 .. U2, C = 0 .. width_in(W)-1.

% Background cells that are above the original height but below the block
bg_start(B) :- B = height_in(H1).
bg_end(E)   :- E = height_out(H) - N - 1.
output(R,C,0) :- height_out(H), zero_count(C,N), R = B .. E, C = 0 .. width_in(W)-1.

% -------------------------------------------------
% Optional: enforce exactly one answer per cell.
% Define a helper predicate for every cell that must appear in the output.
cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).
:- 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% Ensure uniqueness for each cell.
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% -------------------------------------------------
% End of program.

% Auxiliary predicates

column(C) :- input(_,C,_).

height_in(Hin) :- Hin = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

width_in(Wi) :- Wi = 1 + #max { C : column(C) }.

zero_count(C,Nz) :- Nz = #count { R : input(R,C,0) }, column(C).

max_z(Mz) :- Mz = #max { N : zero_count(C,N) }.

max_val(V) :- V = Hin.
max_val(V) :- V = Mz.

height_out(Hout) :- Hout = #max { V } :- max_val(V).

start_bottom(S) :- S = Mz - Nz, zero_count(C,Nz), max_z(Mz).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Output definition
% Bottom part of each column (the last Z(C) cells) becomes 2
output(R,C,2) :- height_out(Hout), start_bottom(S), R = S .. Hout-1, zero_count(C,Nz), C = 0 .. width_in(Wi)-1.

% Copy the original colour for cells that are above the block
output(R,C,V) :- height_out(Hout), zero_count(C,Nz), input(R,C,V), R = 0 .. Hin-1, R = 0 .. (Hout - Nz - 1), C = 0 .. width_in(Wi)-1.

% Background cells that are above the original height but below the block become 0
bg_start(B) :- B = Hin.
bg_end(E)   :- E = Hout - Nz - 1.
output(R,C,0) :- height_out(Hout), zero_count(C,Nz), bg_start(B), R = B .. E, C = 0 .. width_in(Wi)-1.

% Ensure exactly one colour per cell and uniqueness
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% -------------------------------------------------
% Note: The transformation rule is:
%   • Colour 0 is turned into 2.
%   • All other colours stay unchanged.
%   • In each column the zeros “fall” to the bottom; the column with the most zeros decides the final height.
%   • The bottom Z(C) cells of column C become 2; the cells above keep their original colour, and any rows that are above the original height but below the block become 0.
%   This reproduces the three training examples.

% -------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • Colour 0 is turned into 2.

%   • In each column the zeros “fall” to the bottom.
%   • The column with the most zeros determines the final height.

%   This reproduces the three training examples.
% -------------------------------------------------

height_in(Hin)       :- Hin = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width_in(Wi)         :- Wi = 1 + #max { C : column(C) }.

zero_count(C,Nz)     :- Nz = #count { R : input(R,C,0) }, column(C).

max_val(V)           :- V = Hin.
max_val(V)           :- V = Mz.

height_out(Hout)     :- Hout = #max { V } :- max_val(V).

start_bottom(S)      :- S = Mz - Nz, zero_count(C,Nz), max_z(Mz).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Output definition (exactly one colour per cell)

% bottom part of a column: always colour 2
output(R,C,2) :- height_out(Hout), start_bottom(S), R = S .. Hout-1, zero_count(C,Nz), C = 0 .. width_in(Wi)-1.

copy_end2(Q)      :- Q = Hout - Nz - 1, height_out(Hout), zero_count(C,Nz).
output(R,C,V)     :- height_out(Hout), zero_count(C,Nz), input(R,C,V),
                  R = 0 .. P, R = 0 .. Q, C = 0 .. width_in(Wi)-1.

% background cells that are above the original height but below the block
bg_start(B)       :- B = Hin, height_in(Hin).
bg_end(E)         :- E = Hout - Nz - 1, height_out(Hout), zero_count(C,Nz).
output(R,C,0)     :- height_out(Hout), zero_count(C,Nz), bg_start(B), R = B .. E, C = 0 .. width_in(Wi)-1.

% Ensure exactly one colour for each output cell and uniqueness
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% -------------------------------------------------
% The mapping applied:
%   0 -> 2, everything else unchanged.
% The gravity rule above implements the observed behaviour.
% -------------------------------------------------
